title: Phase Stability of Superfluid $^{3}\mathrm{He}$ in Anisotropic Aerogel
authors: J. W. Scott, D. Park, X. Yuan, W. P. Halperin
abstract: The A and B phases of superfluid 3 He have vector degrees of freedom that reflect their characteristic broken symmetries, respectively chiral and spin-orbit rotation axes. Anisotropic disorder in the superfluid, imbibed in uniformly strained silica aerogel, orients these degrees of freedom, thereby affecting phase stability. These degrees of freedom have been found to spontaneously reorient at a field-independent transition temperature Tx , that can be accounted for with a temperature dependent anisotropic Ginzburg-Landau model.
summary: This paper investigates how the superfluid phases of 3He reorient their internal axes when placed in strained silica aerogel. By using NMR spectroscopy and Ginzburg-Landau theory, the authors show that a specific temperature-dependent transition occurs due to the anisotropy of the disorder. This work provides a framework for understanding how external strain can control the symmetry-breaking axes in a topological superfluid.
main_problem: Understanding the phase stability and the temperature-dependent reorientation (n-flop transition) of the vector degrees of freedom in superfluid 3He when subjected to anisotropic disorder from strained silica aerogel.
main_result: The researchers identified a field-independent transition at temperature Tx where the spin-orbit axis (n-vector) flips, driven by the equality of quadratic-order Ginzburg-Landau invariants, and developed a model to interpret the distortion of the order parameter.
method: The study uses NMR spectroscopy to measure frequency shifts and tip angles, combined with an anisotropic Ginzburg-Landau model and the IISM model for pair-breaking effects.
model_or_system: The system is superfluid 3He (a spin-triplet p-wave superfluid) infused in uniformly strained silica aerogel. The theoretical framework is an anisotropic Ginzburg-Landau free energy functional including quadratic and fourth-order invariants and Zeeman energy.
key_observables: NMR frequency shifts (delta omega), tip angle (beta), transition temperature (Tx), and the A-B phase transition temperature (TAB).
important_parameters: Spin-orbit axis (n), chiral axis (l), strain axis (epsilon), order parameter amplitudes (parallel and perpendicular to strain), pair-breaking parameter (x), and dipole field (HD).
main_assumptions: Assumes a homogeneous average order parameter over the dipole length, neglects spatial variations from the Larkin-Imry-Ma effect, and assumes a homogeneous/isotropic distribution of impurities.
figures_summary: Figure 4 shows calculated NMR frequency shifts vs temperature compared to experimental data; Figure 5 shows the pressure dependence of the transition temperature Tx for various aerogel types.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of vector reorientation in anisotropic disorder, presents the experimental NMR measurements, develops an anisotropic Ginzburg-Landau model to fit the data, and discusses the pressure dependence and implications for phase stability.
why_interesting: The study explores how broken symmetries and topological degrees of freedom (chiral and spin-orbit axes) respond to engineered anisotropic disorder, which is relevant to understanding many-body dynamics in disordered environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Tip angle dependence of NMR precession frequency shift. The two possible modes are ˆn ∥H0 (red) and ˆn ∦H0 (blue) above and below Tx at T/Tc = 0.71 and T/Tc = 0.84 temperatures respectively, in 16% positive strain aerogel[12]. Crosses are experimental data at P = 26.5 bar, H0 = 74.4 mT. Dashed lines represent theoretical calculations incorpo- rating order parameter distortion[13, 14].

FIG. 2. Temperature dependence of the NMR resonance fre- quency shift in 16% positive strain aerogel in the B phase at P = 26.5 bar, H0 = 74.5 mT at tip angle β ≈0. The transi- tion Tx from ˆn ∦H0 to ˆn ∥H0 occurs near 1.26 mK.

FIG. 3. Phase Diagrams for TAB and flop temperature Tx dependence on magnetic field in 16% positive strain aerogel at 26.5 bar, experiment and theory, with the A-phase shaded red and B-phase blue. A fit to the H2 dependence of experimen- tally measured TAB is given by the green dotted line. TAB with weak coupling βi and Tc,∥/Tc,⊥= 0.96 is shown with the blue line[22]. Strong-coupling βi with Tc,∥/Tc,⊥= 0.99 is given by the purple dashed line[22–24]. The flop transition temperature Tx is shown with a purple dash-dot line. Exper- imental TAB is shown with the green triangles, Tx with green crosses.

FIG. 4. Calculated small tip angle (β = 0) NMR frequency shifts ∆ω/2π as a function of temperature for the phase di- agram at H0 = 0.0745 T, P = 26.6 bar, strong coupling βi. The experimentally measured Tab is shown in orange, while the calculated Tab is shown in black. The linear extrapola- tion of the GL coefficent α to α(Tx) based on the pressure dependence of Tc over estimates the frequency shift at low temperatures.

FIG. 5. Pressure P dependence of Tx in preplated and non-preplated positive strain (green preplated 16% positive strained, blue non-preplated 14% positive strain) and nega- tive strain (red non-preplated, orange preplated) aerogels[5, 38, 39]. The pure coherence length, appearing in Eq.3 is a well-established function of pressure[40]. The positive slopes d(Tx/Tc0)/dP are not compatible with the pair breaking pa- rameter x for either the homogeneous or inhomogeneous scat- tering models[22, 33]. The black dotted line corresponds to the prediction for the anisotropic IISM model given by equa- tion Eq. 3 for the data point taken in preplated 16% positively strained aerogel at P ≈26.5, bar.
